title: Probing the Valley-Selective Tunneling Density of States in Monolayer MoS2 based Resonant Tunneling Devices
authors: Abir Mukherjee, Kajal Sharma, Ajit K Katiyar, Saranya Das, Samit K Ray, Samaresh Das
abstract: The present work experimentally demonstrates the fabrication of CVD grown monolayer MoS2 ultra thin quantum well based double barrier resonant tunneling device (RTD) architecture well compatible with conventional CMOS fabrication technology. The strongly quantized electronic states from multiple valleys in the momentum space in such ultra 2D sheet along the c-axis sandwiched in between Al2O3 tunneling barriers exhibit multiple resonant tunneling peaks thereby enhancing the FWHM of the NDR region as derived from experimental I-V characteristics as well as theoretical joint invision through Density Functional Theory (DFT) and Non-Equilibrium Greens function (NEGF) visualized via Tunneling Density of States (TDOS). Understanding extended to S-vacancies not only change the bandgap, as evaluated through nanoscale Cathodoluminescence (CL) spectroscopy, but also alters the effective mass hence the mobility as investigated here within the high symmetry path in the k-space. Electrical performances of fabricated RTD, starting from cryogenic to room temperatures, show a significant milestone via exhibiting huge PVR values of 178 at 4K and 24 at RT with more possible improvement in the field of room temperature quantum technology. Momentum conserved and non conserved tunneling from highly n-doped Si through multiple valleys of 1L-MoS2 provides a tremendous opportunity in gate-induced manipulation in Spin-Valley Qubit technology operational at deep cryogenic temperatures (mK).
summary: This paper demonstrates the fabrication of a CMOS-compatible resonant tunneling device using monolayer MoS2 that exhibits high peak-to-valley ratios. By combining experimental transport measurements with DFT and NEGF simulations, the authors show how valley-selective tunneling can be controlled. This work provides a foundation for developing room-temperature quantum technologies and spin-valley qubits.
main_problem: The study aims to develop CMOS-compatible 2D-based Resonant Tunneling Devices (RTDs) and investigate valley-selective tunneling density of states in monolayer MoS2 for potential use in spin-valley qubit technology.
main_result: The researchers demonstrated a high Peak-to-Valley Ratio (PVR) of 178 at 4K and 24 at room temperature, showing that gate-voltage-induced manipulation of momentum-conserved and non-conserved tunneling is possible.
method: The work combines experimental fabrication (CVD growth, e-beam evaporation, and transfer) and characterization (Raman, AFM, XPS, CL, and I-V measurements) with theoretical modeling using Density Functional Theory (DFT) and Non-Equilibrium Green's Function (NEGF) frameworks.
model_or_system: The physical system is a double-barrier resonant tunneling device consisting of a CVD-grown monolayer MoS2 quantum well sandwiched between Al2O3 tunneling barriers, integrated with highly n-doped Si reservoirs.
key_observables: Peak-to-Valley Ratio (PVR), Negative Differential Resistance (NDR), Tunneling Density of States (TDOS), and I-V characteristics.
important_parameters: Temperature regimes from 4K to 300K, MoS2 effective mass (0.46m0), Si effective mass (0.19m0), and sulfur vacancy concentrations.
main_assumptions: The theoretical model utilizes an effective mass approximation and a mode space approach to reduce computational burden.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows Raman spectra, Raman maps, micro-PL, CL characterization, and AFM height estimation; Figure 2 displays high-resolution XPS spectra for elemental composition and valence band analysis.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the fabrication of MoS2 RTDs, details the experimental characterization of material quality and defects, presents the electrical transport measurements (I-V, NDR, PVR), provides theoretical validation via DFT and NEGF simulations of the tunneling density of states, and concludes with applications in spin-valley qubit technology.
why_interesting: The paper explores the manipulation of valley-selective transport and spin-valley states via gate voltage, which is a fundamental concept for developing 2D-material-based quantum information processing and spin-valley qubits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
